mission.title: Install and Configure Prometheus Operator / kube-prometheus-stack
mission.description: The Prometheus Operator simplifies the deployment and management of Prometheus monitoring stacks on Kubernetes. It allows users to leverage Kubernetes custom resources for configuring Prometheus and related components, making it easier to manage monitoring in a cloud-native environment. Key trade-offs include the need for familiarity with Kubernetes custom resources and potential complexity in advanced configurations.

steps.title: Add the Prometheus Operator Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Prometheus Operator Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add prometheus-community https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-charts

helm repo update

```

> This is the official repository for the Prometheus Operator.
> Updates your local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install the Prometheus Operator
steps.description:
This command installs the Prometheus Operator using Helm, specifying the namespace and version.

```bash

kubectl create namespace monitoring

helm install prometheus-operator prometheus-community/kube-prometheus-stack --namespace monitoring --version 0.75.0

```

> Create a namespace for the monitoring stack.
> Install the kube-prometheus-stack with a specific version.

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Prometheus Operator and its components are running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n monitoring

kubectl get svc -n monitoring

```

> List all pods in the monitoring namespace to verify they are running.
> List all services in the monitoring namespace to check service availability.

steps.title: Create a Prometheus custom resource
steps.description:
This step creates a basic Prometheus instance using a custom resource definition.

```bash

cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: Prometheus
metadata:
  name: example-prometheus
  namespace: monitoring
spec:
  replicas: 1
  serviceAccountName: prometheus-operator
  resources:
    requests:
      cpu: 100m
      memory: 400Mi
EOF

```

> This creates a simple Prometheus instance.
commands:
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1
kind: Prometheus
metadata:
  name: example-prometheus
  namespace: monitoring
spec:
  replicas: 1
  serviceAccountName: prometheus-operator
  resources:
    requests:
      cpu: 100m
      memory: 400Mi
EOF

steps.title: Verify Prometheus custom resource
steps.description:
Ensure that the Prometheus instance is running and ready.

```bash

kubectl get prometheus -n monitoring

```

> Check the status of the Prometheus custom resource.

uninstall.title: Uninstall the Prometheus Operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the Prometheus Operator and all associated resources. Be aware that this will delete all monitoring data and configurations.

```bash

helm uninstall prometheus-operator --namespace monitoring

kubectl delete namespace monitoring

```

> Uninstalls the Prometheus Operator.
> Deletes the monitoring namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the Prometheus Operator
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your configurations and data. This step includes draining the Prometheus pods if necessary.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n monitoring -l app=prometheus-operator -o name | xargs -I {} kubectl drain {} --ignore-daemonsets

helm upgrade prometheus-operator prometheus-community/kube-prometheus-stack --namespace monitoring --version 0.76.0

kubectl get pods -n monitoring

```

> Drain the Prometheus pods to prepare for upgrade.
> Upgrade to a new version of the Prometheus Operator.
> Verify that the upgraded pods are running.
commands: kubectl get pods -n monitoring -l app=prometheus-operator -o name | xargs -I {} kubectl drain {} --ignore-daemonsets

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Error creating: pods "prometheus-operator-" is forbidden: error looking up service account monitoring/prometheus-operator: serviceaccount "prometheus-operator" not found
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The service account required for the Prometheus Operator is missing.

**Fix:**
Create the service account using: kubectl create serviceaccount prometheus-operator -n monitoring

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to pull image "docker.io/prometheus/alertmanager:v0.24.0": rpc error: code = NotFound desc = manifest for docker.io/prometheus/alertmanager:v0.24.0 not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The specified image version does not exist.

**Fix:**
Check the available versions and update your configuration to use a valid image version.

**Affected versions:** <= 0.75

troubleshooting.title: Prometheus is not scraping targets.
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The service discovery configuration may be incorrect.

**Fix:**
Verify the service annotations and ensure the targets are correctly labeled for Prometheus to discover.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'kubectl apply -f prometheus.yaml' results in 'error validating data: [apiVersion not set, kind not set]'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The YAML file is missing required fields.

**Fix:**
Ensure that the YAML file has the correct apiVersion and kind specified.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for custom metrics API, improved Grafana dashboards.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated the use of legacy alerting rules.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v0.75, review the new custom metrics API documentation for changes in configuration.

resolution.summary: You should see the Prometheus UI accessible at http://<prometheus-service-ip>:9090, and the status of your Prometheus instance should show 'UP' when running kubectl get prometheus -n monitoring.

kind: Prometheus
metadata:
  name: example-prometheus
  namespace: monitoring
spec:
  replicas: 1
  serviceAccountName: prometheus-operator
  resources:
    requests:
      cpu: 100m
      memory: 400Mi
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with sufficient resources.
